name: Vermont
tagline: A new AI Economic Task Force, a centralizing 'One ADS' tech strategy, a $70M+ Workday HR/finance overhaul, and a near-complete fiber broadband buildout — small-state Vermont is consolidating how it buys and runs technology.

execSummary.summary: Vermont is a small, centralized market where most state technology decisions run through a single agency — the Agency of Digital Services (ADS), led by Secretary & CIO Denise Reilly-Hughes. Three shifts matter for a Dell seller in 2026. First, ADS is executing a multi-year 'One ADS' strategy that moves the state to a service-based (internal service fund) IT budgeting model, backed by a $15M appropriation, to consolidate fragmented systems and bill agencies for shared services. Second, Governor Scott's new AI Economic Task Force (Executive Order 04-26, signed May 18, 2026), which works with Reilly-Hughes, sits alongside the state's existing Council on Artificial Intelligence (Act 132 of 2022) and is tasked within 90 days with naming priority opportunities where AI can help state employees. Third, a $70M+ Workday HR/finance modernization is mid-flight — recently shifted from phased to all-at-once rollout — and Vermont's federally funded fiber broadband buildout is pushing access toward 99% statewide. Buying runs through Buildings & General Services (BGS) Office of Purchasing & Contracting and ADS vendor procurement, increasingly via the VT Buys platform and statewide IT contracts.
opportunities: Infrastructure consolidation under 'One ADS' and the service-based IT funding model — compute, storage, networking, and lifecycle for shared state services.
Governed AI enablement as the AI Economic Task Force and Council on AI define state use cases — an opening for private/on-prem AI on sensitive workloads.
Cybersecurity for state and local government via the Cybersecurity Advisory Council and federal SLCGP grants — secure compute, endpoint, and resilience.
Client/endpoint and data-center refresh tied to the Workday modernization and a record all-funds budget.

stats.label: Service-based IT funding
stats.note: Toward 'One ADS' internal service fund model

budget.intro: Vermont budgets on an annual fiscal-year cycle (July 1–June 30). The FY2026 Appropriations Act (H.493 / Act 27) totals roughly $9.1B across all funds, with about $2.48B in General Fund appropriations. There is no single consolidated 'state IT budget' line — the state spends well over $140M a year on government IT, distributed across agencies, with ADS now moving toward a service-based internal service fund that bills agencies for shared technology.

breakdown.label: Workday HR/finance modernization
breakdown.note: Multi-year; cost may rise with rollout change

notes: Figures are annual (FY2026) unless a program is noted as multi-year/cumulative.
Most technology spend is embedded in agency budgets; the 'One ADS' service-based model will reshape how shared IT is funded and billed — confirm which entity owns a program before forecasting.

FY2027 (governor's proposed, per NASBO): $2.53B general fund, -0.6% vs FY2026; $9.4B all funds. Fiscal discipline amid tighter conditions and less expected federal support; reserves ~$375M (~14.8% of GF).

initiatives.name: One ADS — Service-Based IT Strategy
initiatives.summary: ADS is executing a multi-year 'One ADS' strategy to consolidate fragmented state technology and move to a service-based (internal service fund) budgeting and billing model, backed by a $15M appropriation. The goal is greater reliability, transparency, and predictable cost — a consolidation motion that creates demand for standardized infrastructure, shared compute/storage, and lifecycle services.

initiatives.name: AI Economic Task Force & Council on AI
initiatives.summary: Governor Scott's Executive Order 04-26 (May 18, 2026) created the AI Economic Task Force, working with ADS Secretary/CIO Reilly-Hughes, with working groups on Economic Acceleration, Small Business Competitiveness, and Community Resilience. Within 90 days it must name up to five priority opportunities for AI to help state employees. It complements the existing Council on Artificial Intelligence (Act 132 of 2022) that governs state AI use — an opening for governed, auditable AI.

initiatives.name: Workday HR & Finance Modernization
initiatives.summary: Vermont is replacing legacy human-resources and finance systems with the Workday cloud platform at a cost exceeding $70M. In April 2026 officials postponed the HR (HCM) go-live and moved to a single 'unified launch' now targeted for 2028 after finding the HR and finance components more interconnected than expected; legislators flagged the lack of an upfront gap analysis and are scrutinizing the project — raising cost, risk, and oversight attention. While the platform is SaaS, the slipping timeline opens room for advisory/integration and the surrounding infrastructure and endpoint refresh.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity Advisory Council & SLCGP
initiatives.summary: Vermont's Cybersecurity Advisory Council is extending protection beyond state government to critical-infrastructure and local entities, using a tiered plan (training/planning, risk management, information sharing). Federal State and Local Cybersecurity Grant Program (SLCGP) funds, administered with Ready Vermont, help municipalities meet baseline security — a channel for secure compute, endpoint, and resilience offerings into the SLG market.

initiatives.name: Broadband Buildout — VCBB & CUDs
initiatives.summary: The Vermont Community Broadband Board (VCBB) and locally governed Communications Union Districts (CUDs) are building fiber into rural Vermont with a ~$229M, BEAD-anchored program; NEK Broadband alone won ~$93M to reach ~10,000 addresses. The goal is ~99% statewide access. Primarily a fiber/ISP motion, but the expanding state and CUD digital footprint drives downstream network, edge, and security needs.

procurement.intro: Vermont technology buying is centralized. Statewide purchasing policy and contracting run through the Buildings & General Services (BGS) Office of Purchasing & Contracting, while the Agency of Digital Services (ADS) owns technology strategy, standards, and IT vendor procurement. The state is standardizing sourcing on the VT Buys eProcurement platform, and maintains statewide IT contracts that agencies buy from without running their own solicitations.

vehicles.name: Statewide IT contracts (ITS75 / IT Retainer)
vehicles.detail: Pre-competed statewide agreements for software, resellers, and IT professional services with set rates; agencies purchase against them. Confirm Dell and reseller coverage is current and correctly scoped.

vehicles.name: VT Buys (eProcurement)
vehicles.detail: The state's emerging primary platform for sourcing, solicitation, contracting, and supplier management — register and stay current here.

vehicles.name: ADS vendor procurement
vehicles.detail: ADS leads technology-specific solicitations and standards; engage early to shape requirements for consolidation and AI/security workloads.

entities.name: Agency of Digital Services (ADS)
entities.role: Central IT, cybersecurity, telecom, and standards for all of state government — owns the statewide technology motion.

entities.name: BGS Office of Purchasing & Contracting
entities.role: Statewide purchasing policy, contracting, and the VT Buys platform.

entities.name: Agency of Administration / Finance & Management
entities.role: Budget, the Workday modernization, and finance systems oversight.

entities.name: University of Vermont (UVM) & UVM Health Network
entities.role: Independent research-computing, data-center, and security buyer; UVM Health Network sits on the Cybersecurity Advisory Council.

procurement.competition: Vermont is a small, relationship-driven, centralized market. Volume is modest versus large states, so differentiation comes from fit with ADS consolidation, governed AI, and state/local cybersecurity — plus presence on current statewide IT contracts and VT Buys — rather than price alone. Incumbent resellers and SaaS platforms (e.g., Workday for ERP) hold key footholds.

powerStructure.name: Phil Scott
powerStructure.title: Governor
powerStructure.note: Established the AI Economic Task Force (EO 04-26); sets the administration's technology and economic agenda. Filed May 28, 2026 for a sixth term (2026 is a gubernatorial election year); polls as the most popular U.S. governor and is favored — expect continuity if re-elected.

powerStructure.name: Denise Reilly-Hughes
powerStructure.title: Secretary & State CIO
powerStructure.note: Leads all state IT and cybersecurity; works with the AI Economic Task Force on state-employee AI opportunities. The single most important account contact.

powerStructure.name: John Toney
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
powerStructure.note: State CISO (appointed 2024; current per 2026 sources). Priorities include critical-infrastructure/water-utility protection, CISA-aligned compliance, and consolidating the state's security-tool stack — a fit for secure compute, endpoint, and vendor rationalization.

powerStructure.name: Sarah Clark
powerStructure.title: Secretary of Administration
powerStructure.note: Oversees the budget and the $70M+ Workday HR/finance (ERP) modernization.

powerStructure.name: Cybersecurity Advisory Council
powerStructure.title: Statewide security governance
powerStructure.note: Coordinates state, local, and critical-infrastructure cyber; gateway for SLG security demand.

powerStructure.name: Joint Information Technology Oversight Committee (JITOC)
powerStructure.title: Legislative IT oversight
powerStructure.note: Reviews large IT projects (including Workday) — useful for reading project risk and direction.

news.title: Vermont enacts comprehensive privacy law (S.71) and data-broker overhaul (H.211/Act 138)
news.impact: Signed June 16, 2026; comprehensive law effective Jan 1, 2028. Raises statewide data-governance, privacy, and security requirements — a tailwind for governed data, security, and compliance-ready infrastructure.

news.title: Gov. Scott files for a sixth term (2026 gubernatorial election year)
news.impact: Filed May 28, 2026; polls as the most popular U.S. governor and is favored. Expect continuity of the ADS/'One ADS' and AI agenda if re-elected — but budgets and leadership could shift post-election.

news.title: Gov. Scott signs FY2027 budget (H.951, ~$9.33B); vetoes S.190
news.impact: Budget enacted ~$1M below the January proposal with a 'disciplined spending' posture — expect cost scrutiny on new IT spend.

news.title: Gov. Scott establishes Vermont AI Economic Task Force (EO 01-26)
news.impact: New state AI agenda; ADS co-chairs and must name priority AI opportunities within 90 days (report due ~mid-Aug 2026).

news.title: Vermont postpones Workday HR go-live; unified launch slips to 2028
news.impact: HR/finance components found more interconnected than expected; raises cost/risk, oversight attention, and integration/advisory openings.

news.title: ADS 2026 Annual Report details 'One ADS' service-based IT model
news.impact: Consolidation and internal-service-fund billing reshape how shared technology is funded.

dellActions: Build the relationship with ADS (Secretary/CIO Reilly-Hughes and team) as the single front door — Vermont's IT decisions are centralized there.
Tie infrastructure, compute, and lifecycle offerings to the 'One ADS' consolidation and service-based funding model rather than one-off agency deals.
Lead with governed, private/on-prem AI for sensitive workloads as the AI Economic Task Force and Council on AI define state use cases.
Pursue state and local cybersecurity through the Cybersecurity Advisory Council and SLCGP grant funding — secure compute, endpoint, and resilience.
Confirm Dell and reseller coverage on current statewide IT contracts (ITS75 / IT Retainer) and register/stay current on the VT Buys platform.
